description: BUIDL is a tokenized money market fund managed by BlackRock and administered by Securitize. It provides qualified institutional investors with on-chain exposure to a portfolio of US Treasury bills, cash, and US government-backed repurchase agreements. BUIDL was among the first tokenized funds launched by a major traditional asset manager and rapidly became one of the largest tokenized fund products by TVL. The fund token accrues daily yield, which is distributed monthly as new tokens. Access is restricted to qualified investors who pass KYC/AML onboarding through Securitize's platform.

legal_jurisdictional.rationale: BUIDL operates under a robust multi-tiered regulatory framework. The fund is structured under SEC Rule 506(c) and Section 3(c)(7) of the Investment Company Act—an exemption framework designed for qualified institutional investors. Securitize, LLC (transfer agent) is an SEC-registered broker-dealer and ATS operator. Bank of New York Mellon is a federally chartered bank subject to Fed/OCC oversight, serving as both custodian and fund administrator. KYC/AML onboarding is implemented via Securitize Markets. No material jurisdictional gaps exist for qualified institutional access.

description: Tether Gold (XAUT) is a tokenized gold product issued by TG Commodities Limited, a BVI-incorporated subsidiary of Tether Holdings Limited. Each XAUT token represents one troy ounce of physical gold on a specific LBMA Good Delivery gold bar stored in a Swiss vault. Token holders can claim physical delivery of gold bars of sufficient size or redeem for cash equivalent. XAUT is one of the oldest and largest tokenized gold products by market capitalisation.

legal_jurisdictional.rationale: XAUT is classified as a commodity-backed token (not a security) across major jurisdictions: FINMA (Switzerland) treats it as a payment token satisfying standard commodity vault standards; the FCA (UK) applies AML/KYC frameworks rather than securities law; U.S. CFTC/SEC do not require a commodity token licence for tokenised physical gold storage. Gold is held in Switzerland (Brinks, Loomis)—internationally recognised custodians in a jurisdiction with an established commodity token framework. Commodity tokenisation faces substantially less regulatory friction than security tokenisation.

citations.title: Tether Gold — Terms and Conditions and Legal Framework

valuation_oracles.rationale: Gold spot pricing is continuously quoted via LBMA benchmark rates and multiple independent market providers—no proprietary oracle required. BDO Italia (Big Four) provides quarterly limited-assurance attestations verifying physical gold bar inventory, serial number reconciliation, weight verification, and insurance coverage. However, quarterly attestation frequency lags the monthly cadence offered by competitor PAXG (via KPMG). Some investors criticise partial redaction of vault addresses and bar serial numbers in BDO reports. Tether's historical USDT reserve controversies (2017–2021) are legally separate from XAUT but create residual perception risk.

citations.title: BDO Italia — Tether Gold Quarterly Attestation Reports

custody_asset_control.rationale: Physical gold is stored in Swiss vaults operated by Brinks Global Services and Loomis AB—leading internationally recognised vault operators with military-grade security and standard precious metals insurance. Each XAUT token is individually allocated to a specific LBMA Good Delivery bar serial number, eliminating commingled pooling risk. TG Commodities (as registered vault owner) holds gold at Brinks/Loomis in segregated accounts separate from Tether operational funds. In a worst-case Tether insolvency, gold is physically segregated and XAUT holders would have claims against the custodied assets. Zero custody fees is an advantage over competitors.

citations.title: Tether Gold — Gold Bar Verification and Custody

kyc_aml_permissioning.rationale: Unlike BUIDL and OUSG, XAUT tokens are freely transferable peer-to-peer on Ethereum and TRON without on-chain whitelist controls—any wallet address can hold or transfer XAUT without upfront KYC. KYC is required only at redemption (physical delivery or cash equivalent). This permissive design introduces sanction evasion risk if a sanctioned party acquires XAUT on secondary markets. Additionally, Tether Holdings (parent entity) has historically faced regulatory scrutiny over USDT reserves; while XAUT is legally separate (TG Commodities Limited), regulatory action against Tether's operating entity could disrupt XAUT minting and transferability.

citations.title: Tether Gold — KYC and Verification Policy

secondary_market_liquidity.rationale: XAUT trades on major centralised exchanges (Coinbase, Binance, Gate.io) with over $300M daily trading volume and tight bid-ask spreads for institutional sizes. Redemption is available for 50 XAUT minimum (fractional) or 430 XAUT (one full London Good Delivery bar); cash redemption is also available. Market risk is gold price exposure (annual volatility ~10–15%)—expected and well-understood commodity risk, not idiosyncratic. No major redemption waves or liquidity crises since 2020 launch.

citations.title: Tether Gold — Redemption and Market Information

description: TrueUSD (TUSD) was launched in March 2018 by TrustToken, Inc. as one of the earliest USD-collateralised stablecoins. In late 2022, operational control was transferred to Techteryx Ltd (BVI), an entity whose beneficial ownership has not been fully disclosed publicly. Multiple sources subsequently reported connections between Techteryx and Justin Sun, founder of TRON (who separately faces SEC enforcement for alleged securities violations involving TRX and other tokens). In June 2023, Prime Trust—the Nevada trust company that had been handling TUSD's reserve custody and new minting flows—was placed into receivership by Nevada regulators. Simultaneously, Armanino LLP, which had been providing near-real-time reserve attestations, had already exited the crypto attestation market in November 2022. The combination of opaque new ownership, custodian failure, and the loss of the primary attestation provider created material and unresolved transparency gaps as of 2026.

Multiple outlets (Wall Street Journal, CoinDesk, Bloomberg) have reported connections between Techteryx and Justin Sun. The SEC filed civil charges against Justin Sun in March 2023 alleging unregistered securities sales and market manipulation involving other tokens (TRX, BTT, JST, WIN); TUSD itself was not named, but the regulatory cloud over the associated persons raises governance concerns. TUSD operates without a U.S. securities registration, a trust company charter, or a comparable regulatory anchor. The issuer's jurisdictional framework—BVI incorporation, TRON-chain integration, and opaque ownership—provides investors with substantially weaker legal recourse than competitors operating under NYDFS trust company charters (Paxos) or 1940 Act registration (BENJI, WTGXX).

valuation_oracles.rationale: TrueUSD historically relied on near-real-time third-party attestations to demonstrate 1:1 USD backing. In November 2022, Armanino LLP—then TUSD's primary attestation provider—announced it was exiting the cryptocurrency attestation market entirely. Following Armanino's departure, attestation coverage became irregular. Reporting by blockchain analytics firms in 2023 identified periods in which TUSD reserve attestations were delayed, incomplete, or absent. A January 2024 report by on-chain analytics firms noted that a significant share of TUSD reserves had been placed in offshore or non-liquid investments inconsistent with a cash-equivalent stablecoin—claims that were not conclusively rebutted by publicly available attestation data. No Big Four or equivalent accounting firm currently provides regular independent attestations for TUSD. The absence of credible, continuous reserve verification is a fundamental gap for a USD-pegged stablecoin.

citations.title: Armanino LLP — Exit from Cryptocurrency Attestation Services (November 2022)

custody_asset_control.rationale: Prime Trust, LLC—the Nevada state-chartered trust company that had been processing TUSD's USD inflows and managing reserve custody—was placed into receivership by the Nevada Department of Financial Institutions in June 2023. The receivership was triggered by a shortfall in Prime Trust's own customer assets; TUSD reserves held at Prime Trust were caught in the disruption. New minting of TUSD was suspended during the Prime Trust crisis. Subsequent public disclosures have not clearly identified which qualified custodian(s) hold TUSD reserves today, or under what regulatory framework those custodians operate. The contrast with benchmark peers is stark: BUIDL uses BNY Mellon (federally chartered OCC bank), BENJI uses J.P. Morgan, and PAXG is directly supervised by NYDFS. TUSD's reserve custody arrangements as of 2026 are insufficiently transparent to assess with confidence.

citations.title: Nevada DFI — Prime Trust, LLC Receivership Order (June 2023)
Bloomberg — Prime Trust Placed Into Receivership

kyc_aml_permissioning.rationale: TUSD tokens are freely transferable on-chain across Ethereum, TRON, BNB Chain, Avalanche, and Polygon without on-chain whitelist controls—any wallet address may hold or transfer TUSD without KYC. This is a structural design choice shared with XAUT and PAXG, but the risk is compounded by TUSD's opaque issuer and the absence of a strong regulatory anchor. KYC is applied only at point of primary minting through Techteryx's platform. The TRON deployment is notable: TRON-based TUSD has historically been a popular channel in jurisdictions with capital controls, raising AML risk relative to Ethereum-based stablecoin peers with stricter transfer controls.

citations.title: TrueUSD — Token Overview and Transfer Policy

secondary_market_liquidity.rationale: TUSD retains listings on major centralised exchanges (Binance, OKX, Bybit, Gate.io) and maintains reasonably tight USD pegging under normal conditions. However, the market capitalisation has declined from a peak of approximately $1.4B in mid-2023 to approximately $280M as of early 2026, reflecting a loss of market confidence following the reserve and custody controversies. TUSD has experienced brief depegging episodes—including a period in August 2023 when the token briefly traded at $0.98–$0.99 on secondary markets. Redemption for USD (primary market) requires trusting Techteryx's platform and reserve access, which carries the same opacity risks documented under valuation and custody layers.

citations.title: CoinMarketCap — TrueUSD (TUSD) Market Data

settlement_finality.rationale: TUSD's on-chain mechanics are functional: ERC-20 on Ethereum (approximately 15-second finality), TRC-20 on TRON (approximately 3-second finality), and BEP-20 on BNB Chain. No major smart contract exploits of the TUSD token contracts have been publicly reported. However, governance is now entirely centralised under Techteryx Ltd with no public disclosure of key management, multi-sig structures, or upgrade authority. The contractual ability of Techteryx to pause, freeze, or modify the token—without the regulatory oversight applicable to NYDFS-chartered trust companies or 1940 Act funds—is a meaningful operational risk factor. The Justin Sun affiliation further concentrates governance risk in a person subject to ongoing SEC litigation.

name: Maple Finance — Institutional Credit Marketplace
issuerOrOperator: Maple Labs Inc. (Delaware; protocol developer); individual Pool Delegates (institutional credit managers) administer individual pools
underlyingAsset: Institutional credit positions: primarily undercollateralised loans to crypto-native institutions (historical) and short-duration US Treasuries via Cash Management pools (from 2023); specific collateral varies by pool
domicile: United States (Maple Labs Inc. incorporated in Delaware; protocol contracts deployed on Ethereum and Solana)
tvlNote: Approximate protocol TVL as of early 2026, primarily from the Cash Management USDC pools (backed by US Treasuries) and remaining active credit pools. Protocol TVL peaked near $900M in early 2022, collapsed to approximately $25M by early 2023 following major defaults, then partially recovered. Figure is an approximation; verify via DeFiLlama or Maple Finance protocol dashboard.
description: Maple Finance is an institutional on-chain credit marketplace that enables capital providers to deposit into pools managed by institutional credit delegates, who underwrite and originate loans to crypto-native institutions. Launched in May 2021 on Ethereum, the protocol peaked at approximately $900M TVL in early 2022 and became a primary source of undercollateralised institutional credit in the crypto ecosystem. In December 2022, Orthogonal Trading—a pool borrower—defaulted on approximately $36M in outstanding Maple loans after misrepresenting its exposure to the FTX collapse to pool delegates. Combined with broader 2022 credit stress (Three Arrows Capital-adjacent defaults, Celsius contagion), Maple's TVL fell to approximately $25M by Q1 2023. The protocol subsequently pivoted: Maple v2 introduced stricter collateral requirements, restructured governance, launched the SYRUP rewards token, and added 'Cash Management' pools backed by short-duration US Treasuries. These structural changes have partially restored protocol stability, but the historical default record is a material consideration for prospective participants.

legal_jurisdictional.rationale: Maple Labs Inc. is a Delaware company, and pool delegates typically operate as regulated investment advisers or established institutional credit managers. However, the protocol itself is not registered as a lending institution, investment company, or broker-dealer—the smart contracts mediate lending without a licence from any financial regulator. The legal framework governing undercollateralised on-chain institutional lending remains unsettled across major jurisdictions: neither the SEC, CFTC, nor comparable bodies have issued definitive guidance classifying Maple pool tokens or lending positions. Pool delegates enter loan agreements with borrowers that are legally enforceable off-chain, but jurisdictional enforceability of these agreements against defaulting crypto-native counterparties across multiple jurisdictions has proven difficult in practice—as the Orthogonal Trading default illustrated. The Cash Management pools (Treasury-backed) operate on more conventional legal footing, but these represent only a portion of the protocol.

citations.title: Maple Finance — Protocol Documentation
Maple Finance — Maple v2 Announcement

valuation_oracles.rationale: For Treasury-backed Cash Management pools, NAV is straightforward: US Treasury prices are continuously quoted and third-party fund administrators calculate daily NAV. For credit pools, valuation is entirely dependent on borrower solvency—a fundamentally opaque, non-market-observable input. Pool delegates set loan terms based on private credit underwriting of institutional borrowers; these valuations are not independently verified or publicly disclosed in real time. The Orthogonal Trading default directly exposed this weakness: Orthogonal had represented to pool delegates that it was not materially exposed to FTX; this was false. Pool positions were marked at par until default was declared in December 2022, after which the positions had to be written down to recovery value. There is no centralised NAV oracle that reflects credit quality on a continuous basis; pool token prices on secondary venues (when available) may diverge materially from par.

citations.title: Maple Finance — Orthogonal Trading Default Announcement (December 2022)
CoinDesk — Orthogonal Trading Defaults on $36M Maple Finance Loans

custody_asset_control.rationale: For undercollateralised credit pools—which historically represented the majority of Maple TVL—no physical collateral securing the loan exists on-chain. Capital providers hold an economic claim against the pool smart contract, which in turn holds a legal claim against the borrower. In a default, recovery depends entirely on off-chain legal enforcement against the borrower, which has proven slow and uncertain: as of early 2026, a portion of the Orthogonal Trading default ($36M, December 2022) remains in recovery proceedings. The Maven 11 Capital pool that suffered default write-downs illustrates that even institutional-grade pool delegates cannot guarantee recovery. For Treasury-backed Cash Management pools, the custody picture is better (US Treasury securities held via regulated financial institutions), but the protocol's historic and ongoing credit pools retain the fundamental undercollateralised custody gap. No equivalent of BNY Mellon, J.P. Morgan, or Fireblocks institutional custody exists for unsecured loans.

citations.title: Maple Finance — Orthogonal Trading Default and Recovery Update
Bloomberg — Maple Finance Faces Losses After Orthogonal Trading Default

kyc_aml_permissioning.rationale: Maple Finance is strictly institutional: lenders (capital providers) must complete KYC/AML verification through Maple's platform or the relevant Pool Delegate's onboarding process. Access is limited to accredited investors and qualified institutional buyers in applicable jurisdictions. Borrowers undergo credit due diligence by Pool Delegates, who are typically established asset managers or credit funds (e.g., M11 Credit, Cauris Finance). Geographic restrictions are applied at onboarding. Loan agreements between pools and borrowers are legally enforceable off-chain contracts. Post-default restructuring of the Orthogonal pool included enhanced borrower disclosure and verification requirements. No known KYC or AML enforcement actions against Maple Labs or its pool delegates.

citations.title: Maple Finance — Lender Onboarding Documentation

secondary_market_liquidity.rationale: Maple pool LP tokens have no meaningful secondary market. There are no centralised exchange listings for pool tokens; there is no Uniswap pool with material depth. Liquidity is entirely dependent on the loan repayment cycle: lenders can only exit by requesting withdrawals during designated windows, subject to available liquidity in the pool. During the December 2022 Orthogonal default, positions in the affected pools were effectively frozen—lenders could not redeem until recovery proceedings concluded or protocol governance allocated substitute liquidity. For credit pools with loans still outstanding, withdrawal queues can extend weeks to months. The Cash Management pools offer better liquidity (T+0 or T+1 via Treasury bill liquidation), but these are a minority of historical TVL. Secondary market illiquidity is structural to the undercollateralised lending model, not a transient gap.

citations.title: Maple Finance — Pool Withdrawal Documentation
DeFiLlama — Maple Finance Protocol TVL History

settlement_finality.rationale: On-chain mechanics are sound: Maple pool contracts are ERC-20-based on Ethereum with standard finality (~15 seconds). Smart contracts have been audited by security firms (Trail of Bits, Spearbit, and others); audit reports are publicly available. However, Maple v2 involved a significant protocol migration with new contract deployments—contract migration introduces transition-period risk. Off-chain credit recovery from defaults is the dominant settlement risk: loan repayment ultimately depends on borrower solvency and willingness to pay under legal agreements, which can take years to resolve in adversarial scenarios. The SYRUP token migration from MPL also introduced governance change risk. No major on-chain contract exploits since the 2021 launch, but the protocol has undergone substantial architectural changes following the 2022 default period.

issuerOrOperator: Hamilton Lane Advisors, LLC (SEC-registered investment adviser; NASDAQ: HLNE); Securitize Markets, LLC (SEC-registered broker-dealer and digital transfer agent)
underlyingAsset: Senior secured private credit: primarily senior secured loans and credit facilities to middle-market and large corporate borrowers; exact portfolio composition not publicly disclosed

tvlNote: On-chain TVL / AUM not publicly disclosed. Hamilton Lane's total assets under management and supervision exceed $900B; SCOPE sub-fund AUM is not reported separately in public filings. Verify current figure via Securitize or Hamilton Lane investor relations.
description: Hamilton Lane SCOPE (Senior Credit Opportunities Fund) is a tokenized access vehicle for Hamilton Lane's institutional private credit strategy, made available to accredited investors through a partnership with Securitize. Hamilton Lane (NASDAQ: HLNE) is one of the largest private markets investment managers globally, with over $900B in AUM and supervisory assets. By partnering with Securitize in 2022, Hamilton Lane became one of the earliest major institutional private markets managers to offer tokenized fund access on a public blockchain (Polygon). SCOPE targets senior secured private credit, historically one of the more defensible positions within private credit. However, as a closed-end private fund with institutional underwriting of loans, the product provides limited public transparency about its portfolio, NAV methodology, redemption terms, and secondary market access — characteristics that are standard for private credit but create significant data gaps for RARM assessment.

legal_jurisdictional.rationale: Hamilton Lane Advisors, LLC is an SEC-registered investment adviser and a publicly listed company (NASDAQ: HLNE), subject to extensive public disclosure requirements as a reporting company. Securitize Markets, LLC—the digital transfer agent and distribution partner—is an SEC-registered broker-dealer. The fund is structured as a U.S. private fund, likely under Section 3(c)(1) or 3(c)(7) of the Investment Company Act, restricting access to qualified purchasers or accredited investors. The combination of an SEC-registered public-company investment adviser and an SEC-registered broker-dealer provides a credible and well-regulated distribution and administration framework. Hamilton Lane has no material SEC enforcement history reported as of May 2026.

valuation_oracles.rationale: SCOPE invests in senior secured private credit—an asset class where NAV is calculated through periodic mark-to-model valuations rather than continuous observable market prices. Valuation methodology and the identity of the independent valuation agent have not been publicly disclosed in documents available to this research. Quarterly reporting is standard for institutional private credit funds, but no publicly accessible NAV publication or third-party attestation covering the on-chain token supply against fund shares has been identified. Investors rely on Hamilton Lane's internal valuation committee and any contracted third-party valuation agent; neither the process nor the outputs are publicly verifiable. This is a standard characteristic of the private credit asset class and does not imply deficiency, but insufficient public data prevents an affirmative signal.

Tokens are permissioned via smart contract controls, restricting transfers to KYC-verified wallet addresses. The institutional distribution model and minimum investment threshold (reported to be in the range of tens of thousands of USD; exact figure requires verification via Securitize) provide effective retail access restriction.

Without publicly disclosed redemption terms, secondary market access, or DeFi integration (comparable to ACRED's Morpho collateral integration), the liquidity profile cannot be affirmatively assessed. tvlNote: Approximate on-chain token supply value as of 2024 based on available reporting. STBT reached approximately $150M at peak in 2023 before declining; current figure is a rough estimate. Verify via Matrixdock platform or on-chain data; no real-time public dashboard identified.
description: Matrixdock STBT is a tokenized short-term US Treasury bill product issued by Matrixdock, the institutional investment platform of Matrixport—a Singapore-based digital asset financial services company. STBT is backed by US Treasury bills with maturities under six months and reverse repurchase agreements collateralised by US Treasuries, providing yield exposure approximately aligned to the Federal Funds rate. The product targets non-US professional and institutional investors and has been used as a yield-bearing collateral instrument within DeFi protocols including Curve Finance pools. While the underlying asset class (US T-bills) is among the most transparent and liquid in global finance, Matrixdock's public disclosures—regarding its regulatory licensing status, custodian identity, independent reserve attestations, and on-chain token governance—are significantly less comprehensive than comparable products from NYDFS-regulated issuers (Paxos, WisdomTree), 1940 Act-registered funds (BENJI, WTGXX), or SEC-cleared managers (Ondo OUSG). This creates a meaningful information gap for RARM assessment across multiple dimensions.

legal_jurisdictional.rationale: Matrixport is Singapore-based; however, the specific Monetary Authority of Singapore (MAS) licence(s) applicable to STBT issuance—whether a Capital Markets Services licence, a Major Payment Institution licence, or another MAS authorisation—has not been clearly disclosed in publicly available sources reviewed for this profile. The exact legal entity issuing STBT (Singapore-incorporated, Cayman Islands, or BVI) has not been publicly confirmed. Unlike BENJI (SEC-registered 1940 Act fund), OUSG (SEC investigation closed without charges), PAXG (NYDFS trust company), and WTGXX (SEC exemptive order), STBT has not been subject to publicly disclosed regulatory review or authorisation by a major financial regulator. Matrixport's notable backers include co-founders of Bitmain (a major Bitcoin mining hardware firm); the company has not disclosed any enforcement actions. The gray signal reflects insufficient public regulatory disclosure to positively assess jurisdictional compliance.

citations.title: Matrixdock — STBT Product Overview
MAS — Financial Institutions Directory (search required)

valuation_oracles.rationale: The underlying US Treasury bills are priced continuously via public markets — no proprietary or illiquid oracle is theoretically required. However, no publicly available independent attestation service has been identified that verifies the on-chain STBT token supply against actual off-chain Treasury holdings on a regular (monthly or quarterly) basis. This contrasts with BENJI (PwC annual audit under 1940 Act), PAXG (KPMG monthly attestations), XAUT (BDO Italia quarterly attestations), and WTGXX (Rule 2a-7 NAV). Matrixdock's website references the T-bill backing but does not link to published attestation reports. Without a regular, independent attestation verifying the 1:1 backing, the reserve integrity cannot be affirmatively assessed.

citations.title: Matrixdock — STBT Token and Backing Overview

This contrasts with the explicit custodian disclosures of benchmark peers: BUIDL (BNY Mellon), BENJI (J.P. Morgan), PAXG (Brink's London), OUSG (Fireblocks + Zodia), and WTGXX (WisdomTree Digital Trust Company, NYDFS-chartered). Without knowing whether the custodian is a qualified custodian under applicable regulations, and without published custody agreements or regulatory disclosures, the custody and asset control layer cannot be affirmatively assessed. Gray does not imply a deficiency — Matrixdock may use reputable custodians — but investors require this information to evaluate counterparty risk.

citations.title: Matrixdock — STBT Structure and Collateral

kyc_aml_permissioning.rationale: STBT is targeted at professional and institutional investors outside the United States; initial minting requires KYC/AML verification through Matrixdock's platform. However, it is unclear from public documentation whether STBT tokens carry on-chain transfer restrictions comparable to BUIDL (Securitize whitelist) or BENJI (Securitize whitelist). If STBT tokens are freely transferable after minting (a design shared with XAUT and PAXG), secondary-market holders would not be subject to KYC screening. Matrixdock operates under Singapore-based AML/CFT obligations, but without clarity on on-chain transfer restrictions, a yellow signal is warranted as a precautionary assessment. Yellow indicates a gap relative to best practice (on-chain whitelisting), not confirmed non-compliance.

name: HKSAR Government Digital Green Bond (Project Evergreen)
ticker: HKSAR Digital Green Bond (non-transferable outside CMU settlement system)
issuerOrOperator: Hong Kong Special Administrative Region Government (HKSAR Government); managed and administered by the Hong Kong Monetary Authority (HKMA) on behalf of the Government
underlyingAsset: Direct sovereign obligation of the Hong Kong Special Administrative Region Government; multi-currency fixed-income securities (HKD, USD, EUR, RMB) issued under the Government Sustainable Bond Programme
chainOrPlatform: Goldman Sachs GS DAP (private/permissioned DLT, 2023 inaugural issuance — matured)
domicile: Hong Kong SAR (bonds governed by Hong Kong law; settlement via HKMA Central Moneymarkets Unit)
tvlNote: Approximate outstanding tokenized bond value as of May 2026, representing the November 2025 third issuance (~HK$10 billion ≈ USD 1.28 billion). The February 2023 inaugural issuance (HK$800M, 1-year tenor) matured approximately February 2024. The February 2024 second issuance (~HK$6 billion, 2-year tenor) matured approximately February 2026. Only the November 2025 issuance (2–5 year tenors) is currently outstanding. Figure converted at approximately HKD 7.78/USD.
description: The HKSAR Government Digital Green Bond programme — referred to internally as 'Project Evergreen' by the HKMA — is a landmark series of sovereign tokenized bond issuances by the Hong Kong Special Administrative Region Government, administered by the HKMA. The programme produced three issuances: (1) February 2023: HK$800 million, 1-year, 4.05% coupon, settled on Goldman Sachs GS DAP — the world's first tokenized government green bond, now matured; (2) February 2024: ~HK$6 billion, multi-currency (HKD/RMB/USD/EUR), 2-year, settled on HSBC Orion — the world's first multi-currency digital bond, now matured (~February 2026); (3) November 2025: ~HK$10 billion, multi-currency (HKD/RMB/USD/EUR), 2–5 year tenors, settled on HSBC Orion with e-HKD and e-CNY central bank digital currency settlement for HKD and RMB tranches — the largest digital bond issuance globally. The programme demonstrates the viability of DvP (delivery-versus-payment) settlement for sovereign bonds on distributed ledger technology, with all transactions settled through the HKMA's Central Moneymarkets Unit (CMU). The bonds are restricted to global institutional investors; no retail secondary market exists.

legal_jurisdictional.rationale: The HKSAR Government is the sovereign issuer, representing the strongest possible credit and legal standing within Hong Kong. Bonds are governed by Hong Kong law and cleared through the HKMA's Central Moneymarkets Unit (CMU) — the official central securities depository for Hong Kong. The HKMA, as Hong Kong's de facto central bank, administers the programme directly. The 2023 inaugural issuance established the world's first tokenized government green bond, with the HKMA and Hong Kong Government providing the full institutional and legal framework. Lead managers (Goldman Sachs, HSBC, Bank of China HK, Crédit Agricole CIB, ICBC Asia, UBS, J.P. Morgan, and others) are regulated financial institutions responsible for investor KYC/AML. No regulatory ambiguity — the bonds are conventional HKSAR Government securities with tokenized settlement mechanics, exempt from SFC prospectus authorization under the standard government bond exemption.

citations.title: HKMA Press Release — HKSAR Government's Inaugural Tokenised Green Bond Offering (16 Feb 2023)
HKMA Press Release — HKSAR Government's Third Digital Green Bonds Offering (11 Nov 2025)

valuation_oracles.rationale: The bonds are fixed-coupon sovereign securities with publicly stated coupon rates and maturities: the November 2025 issuance carries coupons of 2.5% (HKD, 2-year), 1.9% (RMB, 5-year), 3.633% (USD, 3-year), and 2.512% (EUR, 4-year). Credit risk is HKSAR Government sovereign risk — rated AA+/Aa3/AA+ — with no default history. Secondary market prices are quoted by institutional dealers and reported through CMU. No proprietary oracle is required; bond pricing is derived from transparent interbank sovereign bond markets. The November 2025 issuance was oversubscribed by more than HK$130 billion across all four tranches, providing strong price discovery evidence.

citations.title: HKMA Press Release — Third Digital Green Bonds: Coupon and Oversubscription Data (11 Nov 2025)
Ledger Insights — Hong Kong Confirms HK$10 Billion Digital Green Bond

custody_asset_control.rationale: Settlement and custody are administered through the HKMA's Central Moneymarkets Unit (CMU) — the official central securities depository of Hong Kong, equivalent in function to the DTC (US), Euroclear (Belgium), or Clearstream (Luxembourg). Institutional investor custodians are major regulated banks (HSBC and Bank of China HK confirmed for the 2023 issuance; leading global custodian banks for subsequent issuances). The private/permissioned DLT settlement layer mirrors CMU's conventional function with additional on-chain DvP mechanics. Sovereign bonds held via CMU carry the full legal protections of Hong Kong securities law. No custody incidents, settlement failures, or technical disruptions reported across any of the three issuances.

citations.title: HKMA — Bond Tokenisation in Hong Kong (Technical Report, August 2023)

kyc_aml_permissioning.rationale: Access is restricted to global institutional investors only (asset managers, banks, insurance companies, private banks) via the lead manager banks, each of which is a regulated financial institution with their own KYC/AML obligations. The private/permissioned DLT architecture ensures that only HKMA-approved institutional participants can hold or settle bonds — there is no permissionless secondary transfer. KYC/AML is enforced at the institutional layer by licensed lead managers (Goldman Sachs, HSBC, Bank of China HK, Crédit Agricole CIB, ICBC Asia, UBS, J.P. Morgan, Société Générale, Standard Chartered, BNP Paribas, Bank of Communications). Retail access and anonymous participation are structurally impossible on the permissioned settlement infrastructure.

citations.title: HKMA Press Release — Joint Bookrunners and Investor Eligibility (Feb 2024)

secondary_market_liquidity.rationale: DvP on-chain secondary settlement was demonstrated in the 2023 issuance and continued in subsequent issuances — a technical advance over conventional bond settlement (T+2). However, secondary market liquidity is determined by institutional supply and demand for HKSAR sovereign bonds, which is inherently narrower than highly-traded global sovereign benchmarks (US Treasuries, UK Gilts). The bonds are not listed on any public exchange and are not accessible to retail investors. DeFi collateral integration does not exist. The November 2025 issuance's 2–5 year tenors carry market risk from interest rate movements over their remaining term. Bond liquidity for the oversubscribed ($130B+ demand vs $10B issued) November 2025 issuance is expected to be reasonable among institutional participants, but no public secondary market price data is available.

citations.title: HKMA — Bond Tokenisation in Hong Kong: Secondary Market Settlement (August 2023)
ICMA — Hong Kong's Tokenised Green Bonds (April 2024)

settlement_finality.rationale: The programme pioneered DvP (delivery-versus-payment) settlement on distributed ledger technology for sovereign bonds in HK, demonstrated across all three issuances. The 2023 issuance used Goldman Sachs GS DAP (Daml-based private ledger); the 2024 and 2025 issuances used HSBC Orion, HSBC's proprietary institutional DLT platform. The November 2025 issuance introduced tokenized central bank money (e-HKD for HKD tranche; e-CNY for RMB tranche) for settlement — the most advanced CBDC-integrated sovereign bond settlement documented globally. HKMA published a detailed technical report (August 2023) documenting settlement architecture, DvP mechanics, and on-chain lifecycle management. Both GS DAP and HSBC Orion are institutional-grade platforms developed and operated by top-tier regulated financial institutions. No settlement failures, rollbacks, or on-chain incidents reported across any issuance.

description: HSBC Gold Token (XGT) is a tokenized physical gold product issued by HSBC Hong Kong, launched on 27 March 2024 as the only retail-focused gold token authorized by the Securities and Futures Commission (SFC) at launch. Each XGT token represents 0.001 troy ounce of LBMA Good Delivery gold stored in HSBC's London vault. The product is available exclusively to HSBC HK retail banking customers through the HSBC HK Mobile App and HSBC Online Banking, enabling fractional gold ownership without the need for physical delivery. Built on HSBC Orion — HSBC's proprietary private/permissioned distributed ledger technology — XGT is not deployed on any public blockchain. Peer-to-peer transfers are permitted between verified HSBC HK customers only. By November 2025, HSBC Gold Token exceeded USD 1 billion in cumulative trades and 100,000 transactions, making it Asia's largest retail gold token. In September 2024, HSBC piloted quantum-safe cryptography for the Gold Token, anticipating future cryptographic security requirements.

legal_jurisdictional.rationale: HSBC Gold Token is SFC-authorised as a collective investment scheme (paper gold scheme) under Hong Kong's Securities and Futures Ordinance — the same regulatory classification applied to conventional LBMA paper gold products. HSBC HK is an HKMA-authorised licensed bank and SFC-licensed corporation subject to extensive HK banking and securities law supervision. The SFC authorised the Principal Brochure for the product prior to retail launch. Clifford Chance served as legal adviser on the launch. This is the strongest available retail investment product authorisation in Hong Kong. No pending enforcement actions or regulatory proceedings against HSBC HK or the Gold Token product as of May 2026.

valuation_oracles.rationale: Gold spot price is continuously quoted via LBMA benchmark rates and multiple independent market providers — no proprietary oracle is required. HSBC prices XGT tokens based on LBMA AM/PM fixings, which is standard market practice for paper gold products. However, no independent third-party attestation report verifying that physical gold bar inventory corresponds to outstanding XGT token supply has been publicly identified. This contrasts with competitor products: PAX Gold (PAXG) publishes monthly KPMG attestation reports verifying bar-level gold inventory, and Tether Gold (XAUT) publishes quarterly BDO attestations. HSBC as issuer is both the gold custodian and the sole verifier of the backing, without a published external audit trail. The SFC authorisation process likely reviewed reserve arrangements, but the outputs are not publicly available.

custody_asset_control.rationale: Physical gold is stored in HSBC's own London vault — an established institutional-grade precious metals facility. However, HSBC simultaneously serves as issuer, platform operator (HSBC Orion), physical custodian, and transfer agent — creating a significant concentration of roles in a single counterparty not replicated by leading global competitors. PAXG holds gold at Brink's Global Services (independent third-party custodian) and is subject to NYDFS supervision. XAUT holds gold at Brinks and Loomis (independent). HSBC as self-custodian means investors have no independent custodial protection if HSBC's gold business operations were disrupted. XGT tokens are restricted to the HSBC Orion private ledger; peer-to-peer transfers are limited to verified HSBC HK customers, which prevents unauthorised access but also means token holders cannot self-custody outside HSBC's system. No custody incidents or disruptions reported since March 2024 launch.

kyc_aml_permissioning.rationale: HSBC Gold Token is available exclusively to HSBC HK retail customers who hold an active HSBC Investment Account — requiring full HKMA-compliant KYC/AML onboarding at account opening. Peer-to-peer token transfers are restricted to verified HSBC HK customers on HSBC Orion's private permissioned ledger; non-HSBC wallet holders cannot hold XGT. This is one of the most restrictive KYC/AML frameworks among tokenized commodity products globally — in contrast with PAXG and XAUT, which are freely transferable on Ethereum to any wallet address without KYC. HSBC HK is subject to HKMA AML/CFT supervision and the Anti-Money Laundering and Counter-Terrorist Financing Ordinance (AMLO). No secondary market exploitation or sanctions evasion risk from permissionless transfer is possible under this architecture.

secondary_market_liquidity.rationale: There is no open secondary market for XGT. Investors buy from and redeem to HSBC at HSBC's quoted gold price (based on LBMA fixings with a spread). Peer-to-peer transfers are possible between HSBC HK customers but represent a bespoke arrangement rather than a liquid market. XGT cannot be sent to external wallets, listed on exchanges, or used as DeFi collateral — contrasting with PAXG (listed on Coinbase, Binance, Kraken; usable in DeFi) and XAUT (listed on Binance and Gate.io). Gold price market risk (annual volatility approximately 10–15%) is present, as with all gold products. The cumulative trading volume milestone ($1B+ by Nov 2025) reflects active purchase and redemption flows with HSBC, not secondary market price discovery. Redemption to HKD cash or withdrawal instructions follows standard HSBC investment account procedures.

settlement_finality.rationale: XGT operates on HSBC Orion — a private/permissioned DLT developed and operated by HSBC — rather than a public blockchain. Settlement is governed by HSBC's internal systems with institutional-grade infrastructure. No public smart contract audit report for the XGT contract has been identified; nor has HSBC published detailed technical documentation comparable to HKMA's Bond Tokenisation report. The private ledger architecture means on-chain settlement cannot be independently verified by third parties, unlike public-chain assets (PAXG on Ethereum, XAUT on Ethereum/TRON). Positively, in September 2024 HSBC piloted quantum-safe cryptography for Gold Token — demonstrating proactive long-term security planning. HSBC is a systemically important financial institution with strong operational resilience standards, but the absence of public auditability is a structural gap relative to public-chain tokenized commodity peers.

description: ChinaAMC HKD Digital Money Market Fund is the first retail-accessible tokenized money market fund in Asia Pacific, launched on 28 February 2025 by China Asset Management (Hong Kong) Limited — the Hong Kong subsidiary of China AMC, one of China's largest fund managers by AUM. The fund is tokenized on Ethereum (ERC-20) via the Libeara platform, developed by SC Ventures, Standard Chartered's venture arm. Standard Chartered Bank (Hong Kong) serves as tokenization agent and fund administrator; Standard Chartered Trustee (Hong Kong) Limited is the fund custodian. The fund is distributed to retail investors through OSL Digital Securities (SFC-licensed virtual asset trading platform) and Solomon (a securities brokerage), making it uniquely accessible to HK retail investors via SFC-regulated crypto channels. The fund invests in HKD-denominated short-term bank deposits and high-quality money market instruments, targeting yield aligned with HKD interbank rates. Annual management fee is 0.15%; tokenization fee is 0.055%. AUM grew from USD 107 million at launch to approximately USD 500 million, confirming strong retail and institutional demand for the product.

legal_jurisdictional.rationale: The fund is authorised by the SFC as an open-ended collective investment scheme (OEFC) — the standard authorisation framework for publicly offered investment funds in Hong Kong, providing the full investor protection regime of the Securities and Futures Ordinance (SFO). ChinaAMC HK is an SFC-licensed corporation (Type 9: asset management). Standard Chartered Trustee (Hong Kong) Limited is an HKMA-authorised trustee institution. OSL Digital Securities is an SFC-licensed virtual asset trading platform (VATP) — one of only a small number of SFC-licensed VATPs operating in HK. The combination of SFC fund authorisation, SFC-licensed fund manager, HKMA-authorised trustee, and SFC-licensed VATP distributor represents the most comprehensive regulatory coverage available for a tokenized fund product in Hong Kong.

description: USYC (US Yield Coin) is a tokenized short-duration US government securities fund issued by Hashnote International Ltd., a Cayman Islands-domiciled asset management firm founded by former traders from DRW Cumberland, the crypto trading arm of DRW Holdings LLC. The fund holds primarily US Treasury bills and government-backed repurchase agreements, providing institutional investors with on-chain exposure to US government securities yield. USYC is notable for its integration as eligible collateral on Deribit, one of the world's largest crypto derivatives exchanges, making it one of the first tokenized T-bill products accepted as exchange margin collateral. Access is restricted to non-US qualified institutional investors through Hashnote's permissioned onboarding.

legal_jurisdictional.rationale: Hashnote International Ltd. is domiciled in the Cayman Islands, a jurisdiction with a well-established offshore fund regulatory framework under the Cayman Islands Monetary Authority (CIMA). The fund structure — a Cayman exempted fund — is standard for institutional tokenized products and well-understood by institutional investors and counsel. US persons are excluded, limiting SEC registration requirements. The DRW Cumberland founding team brings institutional-grade counterparty credibility. The Deribit integration required legal due diligence by a major exchange, providing third-party validation of the legal framework. Key disclosure gap: specific CIMA registration status, fund offering documents, and terms of the Deribit collateral agreement are not publicly available — the legal framework is directionally sound but less transparent than SEC-registered alternatives (BENJI, WTGXX). Signals would be upgraded upon publication of CIMA registration details and fund offering documents.

Institutional tokenized fund products of this type typically employ a regulated prime broker or custodian bank (e.g. a US-licensed broker-dealer holding T-bills in segregated accounts). The DRW founding team's deep connections to institutional prime brokerage suggest a credible custody arrangement, but the absence of a named, audited custodian in public disclosure is a gap relative to BUIDL (Bank of New York Mellon, explicitly named) and OUSG (US Bank / StoneX). The Deribit integration requires Deribit's own KYC/AML compliance for exchange participants, creating a layered AML control environment. The institutional-only, whitelist-gated structure provides a high level of permissioning. The specific AML framework (CIMA registered compliance officer, FATF-aligned procedures) has not been published for public review but is standard for CIMA-regulated Cayman funds. secondary_market_liquidity.rationale: USYC operates primarily as a direct-issue/direct-redeem product; there are no significant DeFi AMM pools or centralised secondary market venues identified in publicly available sources. The Deribit integration provides a significant secondary utility: USYC held as exchange margin earns yield while collateralising derivatives positions, which incentivises holding rather than secondary trading. TVL concentration in a single collateral use-case (Deribit) creates a concentration risk: a change in Deribit's collateral policy could cause significant redemption pressure. No DEX liquidity or external secondary market venues provide meaningful price discovery outside direct fund redemptions.

No smart contract exploits or operational incidents have been reported since launch. The Arbitrum deployment adds L2 liquidity without introducing bridge risk for Arbitrum-native users (bridging to Ethereum L1 carries standard Arbitrum bridge delay of ~7 days for challenge period, relevant only for cross-layer withdrawals). description: Agora USD (AUSD) is a USD-pegged stablecoin issued by Agora Bermuda Limited, domiciled in Bermuda and licensed under the Bermuda Monetary Authority (BMA) Digital Asset Business Act 2018. The Agora Reserve Fund — holding US Treasury bills, overnight cash, and repurchase agreements — is managed by VanEck Associates Corporation and custodied by State Street Bank and Trust Company, one of the world's largest custodian banks. AUSD maintains a 1:1 USD peg; reserve yield is distributed to ecosystem partners rather than AUSD holders. The stablecoin is deployed across 17 blockchain networks via the LayerZero Omnichain Fungible Token (OFT) standard. Agora's Atlas product enables 24/7 atomic 1:1 instant exchange of AUSD for USDC or USDT. The reserve fund is audited by PricewaterhouseCoopers and monitored continuously on-chain by Chaos Labs Proof of Reserves. Agora filed an OCC national trust bank charter application in April 2026. US persons are excluded from primary issuance.

legal_jurisdictional.rationale: Agora Bermuda Limited holds a Digital Asset Business (DAB) licence under the Bermuda Monetary Authority (BMA), issued under the Digital Asset Business Act 2018 (DABA) — a purpose-built licensing regime for digital asset businesses including stablecoin issuers. The Bermuda DAB framework requires capital adequacy, AML/CFT compliance, and ongoing supervisory oversight from the BMA. The Agora Reserve Fund is structured as a bankruptcy-remote Bermuda entity with segregated account protections, legally isolating reserve assets from claims against Agora's operating company. The reserve is managed by VanEck Associates Corporation, a registered US investment adviser with a decades-long institutional track record, adding further fiduciary credibility. Agora filed an application with the OCC for a national trust bank charter (Agora National Trust Bank) in April 2026, demonstrating proactive engagement with US regulatory requirements. US persons are excluded from primary issuance, limiting SEC registration obligations. The issuer's legal entity, licence type, and fund structure are publicly disclosed. Key gap: the Terms of Use note that 'AUSD redemption is available at par value through September 28, 2026'; this temporal language requires clarification before publication to confirm it does not represent a redemption gate or a fixed programme term.

valuation_oracles.rationale: The Agora Reserve Fund holds US Treasury bills, overnight cash deposits, and repurchase agreements — the highest-quality, most liquid short-duration USD instruments available. AUSD maintains a fixed 1:1 USD peg, not a floating NAV, with all reserve assets valued at cost or market (T-bills). PricewaterhouseCoopers LLP conducts monthly attestations of the reserve fund, verifying that circulating AUSD supply is backed at least 1:1 by reserve assets. Chaos Labs integrated Proof of Reserves (PoR) in May 2025, providing continuous on-chain publication of: (1) total NAV of reserve collateral; (2) circulating on-chain supply; and (3) cross-verification of collateralisation status. This dual-layer approach — off-chain PwC monthly audit plus Chaos Labs continuous on-chain PoR — represents among the most transparent reserve verification arrangements available in the stablecoin sector. The PwC attestation portal returned a 404 during research; the actual attestation report availability should be confirmed before citing specific attestation dates.

kyc_aml_permissioning.rationale: Primary AUSD minting through Agora's Atlas interface requires Know Your Business (KYB) / AML onboarding for institutional clients. US persons are excluded. However, secondary market access to AUSD — via DEX trading on supported chains, exchange listings (Bullish, LBank), and yield protocols (Beefy Finance, Morpho) — appears available without direct KYC through Agora, as noted by Beefy Finance stating 'no geographic restrictions apply' for its AUSD integration. This creates a permissioning gap: while primary minting is gated, secondary holders and DeFi users may access yield generated from a reserve of government securities without Agora-conducted identity verification. The detailed AML procedures — Travel Rule compliance across 17 chains, ongoing monitoring, PEP/sanctions screening cadence — have not been published for independent review. The Bermuda BMA DAB framework mandates AML/CFT compliance, but the compliance documentation for secondary market access is materially less transparent than BUIDL (Securitize, SEC-registered, full whitelist enforcement) or BENJI (SEC-registered investment company). Additionally, the September 28, 2026 redemption language in the Terms of Use requires clarification to confirm redemption rights are not structurally limited.

The enterprise white-label stablecoin product (e.g. GGUSD for Find Satoshi Lab) creates additional adoption vectors. TVL has grown from USD ~0 to USD ~137M within approximately 10 months of launch, demonstrating healthy market uptake. settlement_finality.rationale: AUSD's 17-chain deployment using the LayerZero OFT (Omnichain Fungible Token) standard creates a significantly wider smart contract attack surface than single-chain products. Cross-chain transfers are executed via LayerZero message-passing, introducing LayerZero protocol risk in addition to the per-chain contract risks. The 17 simultaneous chain deployments — spanning EVM chains (Ethereum, Arbitrum, Base, Polygon, Avalanche, BSC, Mantle, Core DAO, Fraxtal, Immutable, Plume, Katana, Monad, Injective) and non-EVM chains (Solana, Sui) — require multi-language audits (Solidity, Rust/Move). Five independent audit firms (Cantina/Spearbit, Certora, Zellic, MoveBit, Halborn) have reviewed the codebase, covering EVM and Move deployments. No exploits have been reported since launch. Per-chain settlement finality is standard for each chain (e.g., ~15 seconds on Ethereum). The aggregate cross-chain architecture is more complex than single-chain peers (TBILL, WTGXX) but is supported by a thorough multi-firm audit programme. The OFT bridge risk is the primary residual concern.

name: Superstate Crypto Carry Fund
issuerOrOperator: Superstate Inc. (investment management transitioning to Bitwise Investment Manager, LLC effective June 1, 2026; on-chain infrastructure operated by Superstate FundOS)
underlyingAsset: Long spot BTC, ETH, SOL, and staked ETH paired with short crypto futures/forwards (CME and institutional prime brokers); unallocated capital in US Treasury bills and cash (basis/carry strategy)
domicile: United States (Superstate Asset Trust, Delaware Statutory Trust; Section 3(c)(7) of the Investment Company Act 1940; Rule 506(c) of Regulation D)
tvlNote: AUM of USD 267 million as of April 30, 2026, per Bitwise/Superstate press release (PR Newswire, 7 May 2026). Superstate does not publish a real-time TVL tracker; this is the most recent independently confirmed figure. The rwa.xyz data page shows a stale lower figure; the PR Newswire figure is preferred.
description: The Superstate Crypto Carry Fund (USCC) is a tokenized actively-managed fund that captures the 'carry' — the persistent premium of cryptocurrency futures prices over spot — by holding long spot positions in BTC, ETH, SOL, and staked ETH while shorting corresponding futures and forward contracts on CME and through institutional prime brokers (including FalconX). The market-neutral strategy targets basis yield without directional crypto price exposure. Unallocated capital is held in US Treasury bills and cash. USCC is structured as a series of the Superstate Asset Trust, a Delaware Statutory Trust, offered exclusively to US Qualified Purchasers under Section 3(c)(7) of the Investment Company Act and Rule 506(c) of Regulation D. Superstate Services LLC, an SEC-registered transfer agent, maintains the KYC-gated on-chain allowlist. Over USD 100 million of USCC assets have been deployed as collateral in DeFi protocols including Aave, Morpho, Kamino, and Spark. In May 2026, Superstate announced that investment management of USCC will transfer to Bitwise Investment Manager, LLC effective June 1, 2026, with Superstate continuing to operate the on-chain tokenization infrastructure via FundOS. USCC was co-founded by Robert Leshner, founder of Compound Finance.

legal_jurisdictional.rationale: USCC operates within a rigorous US regulatory framework. The fund is structured as a series of the Superstate Asset Trust, a Delaware Statutory Trust under the Delaware Statutory Trust Act (DSTA) — providing robust statutory bankruptcy-remoteness: fund assets are legally isolated from claims against Superstate Inc. The offering uses Section 3(c)(7) of the Investment Company Act (Qualified Purchasers only) combined with Rule 506(c) of Regulation D, a standard and well-tested private placement structure for institutional funds. Superstate Inc. is registered as an Exempt Reporting Adviser (ERA) with the SEC (CRD #336188, adviserinfo.sec.gov). Superstate Services LLC is registered with the SEC as a transfer agent — one of very few tokenized fund operators running an SEC-registered digital asset transfer agent — providing a regulated on-chain allowlist. The management transition to Bitwise Investment Manager, LLC (effective June 1, 2026) is a material change: Bitwise is an established registered investment adviser managing over USD 10 billion in assets. The transition is documented publicly and the fund structure (Delaware Statutory Trust, USCC ticker, smart contract addresses) is preserved. No regulatory enforcement actions against Superstate Inc. or Robert Leshner have been identified in public sources [requires confirmation against EDGAR, CFTC, and FinCEN databases before publication].

valuation_oracles.rationale: NAV is calculated daily by NAV Fund Services, an independent fund administrator, based on the mark-to-market of the long spot crypto positions and the offsetting futures/forward shorts. Superstate publishes NAV on-chain via a proprietary Continuous Price Oracle, using linear extrapolation between daily NAV checkpoints to enable intraday atomic subscriptions and redemptions. An independent Chainlink NAVLink feed (USCC NAV per Share on Ethereum mainnet) provides a daily cross-verification reference. The dual-oracle architecture (Superstate custom for intraday, Chainlink for daily verification) is a meaningful transparency measure. However, the underlying is fundamentally different from a T-bill fund: marking the basis position requires pricing crypto futures contracts (CME-listed, and OTC forwards with FalconX), which is inherently more complex and volatile than marking US government securities. The NAV can decline if the futures premium compresses or inverts — USCC is an actively managed strategy fund with a floating NAV, not a fixed-value instrument. The annual EY financial audit covers fund financial statements; the publication and availability of audited financial statements for fiscal year 2024 requires confirmation.

custody_asset_control.rationale: Spot crypto assets (BTC, ETH, SOL, staked ETH) are custodied by Anchorage Digital Bank N.A., the first federally chartered digital asset bank in the US (OCC charter, January 2021). Anchorage holds a SOC 1 Type I attestation (EY-conducted). Fiat cash and USD positions are held at UMB Bank, a regulated US bank. The custody chain for USCC is robust for a crypto-strategy fund. However, two structural risks differentiate USCC from T-bill funds: (1) Crypto asset custody carries inherently different risks than bank custody of government securities — private key management, staking slashing risk on staked ETH positions, and exchange counterparty risk on CME/FalconX futures positions. (2) More than USD 100 million of USCC tokens have been deployed by holders as collateral in DeFi protocols (Aave, Morpho on Ethereum; Kamino on Solana; Spark/MakerDAO ecosystem) — creating layered smart contract risk, liquidation cascade risk, and oracle manipulation exposure separate from and in addition to the fund's own smart contract risk. Smart contract security: 0xMacro conducted an audit in October 2024 (scope: SuperstateToken.sol, SuperstateOracle.sol, MorphoLiquidationV1.sol), finding 3 medium, 2 low, and 5 code quality issues — all medium/low issues resolved; no critical or high-severity findings. ChainSecurity is also listed as an auditor in Superstate's documentation; the scope and findings require direct confirmation.

secondary_market_liquidity.rationale: USCC offers daily liquidity (every market day) for subscriptions and redemptions — meaningfully better than fund peers offering weekly or monthly windows. Redemptions are available in USD (wire) or USDC on Ethereum or Solana, processed via Superstate's Atomic Redemption system. There is no open secondary market or DEX AMM pool for USCC; transfers are restricted to KYC-whitelisted wallets. Despite the absence of a secondary market, over USD 100 million of USCC has been deployed as DeFi collateral (Aave, Morpho, Kamino, Spark), creating indirect secondary-market-equivalent liquidity for holders who collateralise their positions. However, this DeFi collateral deployment introduces a structural liquidity risk: in a stressed redemption scenario, holders with USCC posted as DeFi collateral cannot redeem without first unwinding their lending positions, which may trigger cascading liquidations if redemptions are large relative to the protocol liquidity pools. The basis trading strategy itself requires futures position unwinding to meet redemptions, which in periods of compressed futures premium or high volatility may occur at adverse prices.

description: Ondo US Money Market Fund (OMMF) is a tokenized investment product offered by Ondo Finance, Inc. that provides qualified US investors with on-chain exposure to a US government money market fund. OMMF is structurally similar to Ondo's OUSG product — both are Regulation D private placements restricted to US accredited investors, administered on Ethereum with KYC/AML enforcement through Securitize Markets — but differs in the underlying instrument: OMMF holds shares in a money market fund (targeting USD 1.00 stable NAV with daily accruing yield), whereas OUSG holds shares in a short-duration US Treasury ETF (floating NAV). OMMF is intended for investors seeking stable $1.00 NAV on-chain yield rather than capital appreciation. The specific underlying money market fund manager requires verification against current offering documents. Access is limited to US accredited investors who complete KYC/AML onboarding through Securitize's platform.

legal_jurisdictional.rationale: OMMF is issued by Ondo Finance, Inc., a Delaware corporation, under the same regulatory framework as OUSG: a Regulation D private placement (Rule 506(c)) exempt from SEC registration under Section 3(c)(1) or 3(c)(7) of the Investment Company Act of 1940. Securitize Markets, LLC — an SEC-registered broker-dealer and transfer agent — handles investor onboarding and KYC/AML. The fund structure isolates investor assets from Ondo's corporate balance sheet. The underlying US government money market fund is itself subject to SEC Rule 2a-7 (the federal regulation governing money market funds), adding a second tier of regulatory oversight over the reserve assets. The legal structure is well-precedented and identical in form to OUSG, which has operated without regulatory challenge since its January 2023 launch. Key gap: the specific offering memorandum and the identity of the underlying money market fund (and its manager) require verification against current Ondo documentation before this profile is cited in formal research.

NAV is calculated daily by the underlying fund's administrator. Ondo publishes daily NAV updates for OMMF reflecting the underlying fund's accrued yield. The valuation methodology is among the most transparent and well-regulated of any investment product type. The stable $1.00 target NAV eliminates mark-to-market uncertainty present in floating-NAV products. name: Goldfinch Protocol — Senior Pool (FIDU)
issuerOrOperator: Warbler Labs, Inc. (protocol developer); Goldfinch DAO (governance)
underlyingAsset: Senior pool participation in uncollateralised credit facilities to emerging-market fintech borrowers (Southeast Asia, Sub-Saharan Africa, Latin America)
domicile: United States (Warbler Labs, Inc., Delaware; DAO has no formal legal domicile)
tvlNote: Approximate TVL as of early 2025 per DeFiLlama. Peak TVL exceeded USD 100 million in 2022. TVL declined materially following documented borrower pool defaults in 2022–2023 and subsequent withdrawal restrictions that reduced net redemptions. Current figure requires verification.
description: Goldfinch is an Ethereum-based decentralised credit protocol developed by Warbler Labs, Inc. that provides tokenized exposure to uncollateralised credit facilities extended to emerging-market fintech borrowers. FIDU is the senior pool LP token: holders deposit USDC into a shared senior pool that is automatically allocated across individual borrower pools, with junior tranche Backers absorbing first losses. Borrowers include fintech lenders operating in Southeast Asia, Sub-Saharan Africa, and Latin America. The protocol experienced significant borrower pool defaults in 2022–2023, including Almavest and Cauris Finance, resulting in impairment of some pools and material restrictions on FIDU withdrawal liquidity. As of 2024, FIDU trades at a discount to stated NAV on secondary markets, reflecting the market's assessment of the impaired credit portfolio. GFI is the separate governance token; this profile covers FIDU (the credit exposure token) only.

legal_jurisdictional.rationale: Warbler Labs, Inc. is a Delaware corporation and the primary developer of the Goldfinch protocol. The protocol operates with DAO governance, creating an unclear legal responsibility structure for ongoing obligations. FIDU tokens likely constitute securities under the Howey test, and individual borrower pool tokens may constitute debt securities — neither has received a SEC no-action letter or formal regulatory clearance. The protocol introduced Unique Identity (UID) NFTs via Persona for investor KYC, and has restricted US retail investor access to certain pools, indicating awareness of regulatory risk. Off-chain loan agreements with borrowers in multiple emerging-market jurisdictions (Uganda, Kenya, Nigeria, Philippines, Mexico, Indonesia) are the legal basis for repayment obligations; enforceability varies materially across these jurisdictions. The documented default cases (Almavest, Cauris Finance) have not produced publicised successful legal recovery proceedings, raising questions about cross-border enforcement effectiveness. No formal regulatory enforcement action against Goldfinch or Warbler Labs has been reported, but the regulatory status of the protocol remains unresolved.

citations.title: Goldfinch Protocol — Official Documentation and Whitepaper
Warbler Labs — Goldfinch Protocol Developer

valuation_oracles.rationale: FIDU's on-chain NAV is calculated by the smart contract based on total USDC in the senior pool plus accrued interest, divided by total FIDU supply. Following the documented defaults of Almavest and Cauris Finance (among others, which required verification of specific loss amounts), portions of the senior pool's credit exposure became non-performing. The on-chain NAV calculation does not automatically mark down non-performing loans — impaired loans continue to accrue interest at the contractual rate in the smart contract even when repayment has ceased. This creates a divergence between the stated on-chain FIDU NAV and the economically recoverable value of the portfolio. Secondary markets reflect this: FIDU trades at a significant discount to stated NAV on Uniswap, which is the effective market price discovery mechanism. No independent credit auditor or third-party collateral monitor has been identified for Goldfinch's borrower pools. The valuation architecture — self-reported on-chain NAV that does not automatically reflect credit impairment — is a fundamental risk in protocols of this type.

citations.title: Goldfinch Protocol — FIDU Smart Contract and NAV Mechanics
The Block — Goldfinch Borrower Defaults and FIDU Liquidity Issues

custody_asset_control.rationale: The on-chain components of Goldfinch — USDC held in the senior pool smart contract, FIDU tokens, borrower pool tokens — are governed by audited Ethereum smart contracts (audited by Trail of Bits and OpenZeppelin; specific audit report findings and dates require verification). No major smart contract exploits have been reported. However, the core credit risk lies entirely off-chain: actual loan agreements are bilateral contracts between Goldfinch borrower pools (off-chain entities) and their lenders (on-chain pool investors). The loans are uncollateralised — there are no physical or digital assets backing the credit facilities beyond the borrower's covenant to repay. In cases of borrower default, there is no on-chain mechanism to recover funds; recovery depends entirely on the enforceability of the off-chain loan agreement in the borrower's jurisdiction. This structural feature — on-chain tokens representing off-chain uncollateralised credit risk in emerging markets — is the defining custody risk of the Goldfinch protocol.

citations.title: Goldfinch Protocol — Loan Structure and Borrower Pool Architecture

kyc_aml_permissioning.rationale: Goldfinch introduced Unique Identity (UID) NFTs as on-chain KYC credentials issued via the Persona identity verification platform. FIDU participation in the senior pool requires a UID, and Backer access to individual pools also requires a UID with a higher level of accreditation verification. US retail investor access has been restricted for certain pools; access requirements vary. The DAO governance model means no single legal entity bears formal AML compliance obligations — Warbler Labs provides development and operational support but does not act as a regulated financial intermediary for AML purposes. The borrowers (emerging-market fintechs) conduct their own KYC/AML for their end borrowers, but this is not independently verified by the Goldfinch protocol or Warbler Labs. Travel Rule compliance for on-chain USDC flows has not been confirmed. The UID system is a meaningful improvement over purely permissionless DeFi access, but the decentralised compliance model falls short of regulated intermediary standards.

citations.title: Goldfinch — Unique Identity (UID) KYC System via Persona

secondary_market_liquidity.rationale: FIDU's primary redemption mechanism — withdrawal from the senior pool at stated NAV — has been materially impaired since borrower pool defaults began in 2022–2023. The protocol's withdrawal system processes redemptions from available liquidity (net incoming repayments from active borrowers), but defaults have reduced incoming cash flow relative to withdrawal demand. FIDU holders wishing to exit at or near stated NAV face extended queues or cannot exit at all through the primary mechanism. The secondary market (primarily Uniswap FIDU/USDC pools) provides liquidity but at a significant and persistent discount to the on-chain stated NAV — the discount represents the market's assessment of the portfolio's impairment. This discount effectively means that FIDU holders are locked in at stated NAV or must accept a material loss on secondary market exit. The combination of impaired primary redemptions and significant secondary market discount constitutes a severe liquidity failure for the asset.

citations.title: Goldfinch — Senior Pool Withdrawal Mechanics
CoinDesk — Goldfinch Protocol Defaults and Liquidity Issues

settlement_finality.rationale: Goldfinch smart contracts on Ethereum are audited by Trail of Bits and OpenZeppelin (specific audit scope, findings, and dates require verification against the Goldfinch documentation). No smart contract exploits have been reported; the on-chain infrastructure has performed as designed. On-chain settlement of USDC deposits, FIDU minting, and token transfers is standard Ethereum finality (~15 seconds). However, the protocol's upgrade governance — controlled by the Goldfinch DAO — introduces execution risk for contract changes; DAO-approved upgrades have been necessary to respond to defaults, and the decentralised governance process can slow response to urgent risk management needs. The DAO structure also means no single party can unilaterally pause the protocol in an emergency, unlike centralised platforms with admin keys. No significant governance failures or malicious proposals have been successfully executed.
